segments.s314-g249: This is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic construction: 'سر به رفتن نهاد' literally 'placed (his) head to going' = 'set out / began to go'. 'و' is the simple conjunction 'and' linking from the previous line. 'زآن جایگه' = 'from that place' (ز + آن + جایگه). 'سر' is literal 'head' used in the idiom and is the object of 'نهاد' (نهادن = 'to place'); 'به' marks direction toward the infinitive 'رفتن'; 'نهاد' is the past tense of نهادن used here to mean 'placed/began'. Word order reflects Classical Persian: particle + prepositional phrase + object + infinitive + verb 'put'.
segments.s314-g250: 'همی رفت' is a classical imperfective/past-continuous phrasing: همی (progressive particle) + past form رفت giving 'he was going / he went on'. 'با کام دل' is an instrumental/ablative phrase 'with the heart's desire' = 'with his desire fulfilled' (کام = desire, دل in genitive). 'شاه شاد' states the agent and his state: 'the king (being) glad' — Persian often omits the copula 'بود' so adjective follows the noun to describe it; here it apposes or identifies the subject of رفت.
segments.s314-g177: ز ترکان = a prepositional phrase 'from/among the Turks' (ز used poetically). هر آن کس که introduces a free relative 'whoever' (هر آن کس + که). بد سرفراز is an adjectival phrase: سرفراز 'proud/victorious' qualified by بد as a negative/intensifying qualifier ('badly/over-') — together meaning 'overly/arrogantly proud'. The verb that completes this clause comes in the next segment.
segments.s314-g178: شدند is the simple past plural 'they became' (here used resultatively with the plural or generic subject implied by 'ترکان'/'هر آن کس'). بی‌نیاز is a predicative adjective 'free/independent (of need)'; it takes an از-phrase to mark what one is free of: از نوازش 'from/of favour/affection'. همه ('all') emphasizes 'from everyone's favour' or 'of all favour'. Word order places the verb before the predicative phrase, a common classical/persian poetic order.
segments.s314-g179: به + noun phrases give two adverbial time/place phrases in parallel: به رخشنده روز 'in/at the radiant/bright day' and به هنگام خواب 'at the time of sleep/at bedtime'. رخشنده is an epithet ('radiant/bright') placed before روز, and هنگام + noun is the idiom 'at the time of ...'. These phrases adverbially modify the following verb.
segments.s314-g180: همی is an archaic/homeric adverbial particle meaning 'continually/always' placed before the verb. آگهی is the object 'information/news' placed before the verb (typical Persian order). جست is the past of جستن 'he sought' (here 'sought news'); ز introduces the source 'from Afrasiab'. The subject is omitted and recovered from context (he).
segments.s314-g181: از ایشان ('from them') is a fronted prepositional phrase; کسی ('no one' here) is the indefinite subject used with a negative verb. زو is the enclitic form of از + او (ʻof him/about himʻ) attached before the object نشانی ('sign/trace'). نداد is the simple past negative 'did not give' (classical past form). The clause literally: 'From them no one gave a sign of him.'
segments.s314-g182: This clause is inverted from the more colloquial در جهان نیز از او یاد نکردند ('they did not remember/mention him in the world either'). نِکردند is the past negative 'they did not do' and combines with the noun یاد ('remembrance/mention') to mean 'did not remember/mention'. از او ('of/about him') is the object; در جهان نیز ('even in the world') is an adverbial phrase. The verb agrees with the previously implied plural subject (ایشان).
segments.s314-g183: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'. یک شب ('one night') is a temporal adverbial. سر و تن ('head and body') is a coordinated object (the whole person). بشست is the classical past form of شستن ('he washed'); here it implies he washed himself. Word order follows classical Persian with the verb at the end of the clause.
segments.s314-g184: بشد is a classical/periphrastic past ('it became' / 'he withdrew/was made'); here it marks change of state or movement ('he turned away / withdrew'). دور is an adverb/adjective 'afar, distant'. با دفتر زند is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the دفتر (book) of زند (Zand)' — i.e., the Zand-book or a book named Zand. و است is a poetic copular continuation ('and is / and remained') linking this state to what follows; the copula is present-tense form used in narration.
segments.s314-g25: همی بود is the classical/perfective past progressive: همی marks an ongoing or habitual past and بود is the past copula 'was'; together they mean 'was (continuously)'. در گنگ دژ is a locative prepositional phrase ('in the Gank citadel') placed before the subject شهریار, and شهریار is the nominal subject 'the king' (poetic word order often puts place before subject).
segments.s314-g26: یکی سال is a duration phrase 'one year' (یکی = one/a certain), and با + noun ('با رامش و میگسار') expresses accompaniment or manner ('with tranquillity and with wine-companions'); the coordinating و links the two accompanying nouns. The entire phrase modifies how/for how long the king was in the citadel.
segments.s314-g27: چون here introduces a simile ('like/as'), so 'جهان چون بهشتی دلاویز بود' literally 'the world was like a delightful heaven.' The adjective sequence بهشتی دلاویز stacks two modifiers ('heavenly' + 'charming') before the copula بود (past 'was').
segments.s314-g28: پر از is an idiomatic stative construction meaning 'full of'; از functions as the 'of' in English. The following nouns are a simple coordinated list (گلشن و باغ و پالیز) giving the things the world is full of, with the final بود as the copula repeating/confirming the state in the past.
segments.s314-g29: برفتن is the infinitive 'to depart'; placed at the start as the action under consideration. همی precedes the clause as the progressive/continuous marker. شاه را uses the object/topic marker را with the subject (a common poetic/topic-marking use) and دل نداد is a negated past verbal idiom literally 'did not give heart' meaning 'did not feel like' or 'would not consent'.
segments.s314-g30: همی بود again expresses a continuing past state ('remained'); در گنگ gives the location, and the two adjectives پیروز و شاد are predicative complements joined by و, describing the king's condition ('victorious and glad'). The simple past/copula is implied by بود.
segments.s314-g31: This noun phrase lists participants: همه پهلوانان ایران = 'all the champions of Iran' (the ezāfe linking is omitted in poetry), and سپاه appended names the army as well — a coordinated/appositive grouping 'all the champions and the Iranian army.' Word order reflects poetic compression.
segments.s314-g32: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went/proceeded.' یکسر is an adverb modifying the verb ('at once' / 'together'), and به نزدیک + شاه is a directional phrase 'toward/near the king' (به + near = toward the presence of the king).
segments.s314-g185: 'همه شب' is a temporal phrase meaning 'the whole night' (modifier before noun). 'به پیش' is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before', and 'جهان‌آفرین' ('the Creator') is the noun governed by that phrase.
segments.s314-g186: 'همی بود' is an archaic/poetic habitual/continuous past construction ('he was continually / kept on'); 'گریان' is a predicative adjective 'weeping'; the coordinated phrase 'و سر بر زمین' means 'and (with) [his] head upon the ground' (literal word order: verb + adjective + conjunction + noun phrase).
segments.s314-g187: 'همی گفت' is the habitual/continuous past 'he kept saying' (همی before a past verb gives ongoing repetition). 'کاین' introduces a reported content clause ('that/this'), and 'بنده ناتوان' is a noun plus adjective in the common order (bandeh = humble self-reference here, followed by ناتوان 'incapable/powerless').
segments.s314-g188: The clause places the predicate before the noun in a somewhat poetic order: 'همیشه' ('always') modifies the state, 'پر از درد' is a prepositional phrase 'full of pain', and 'دارد روان' literally 'has (a) soul' → here 'his soul is full of pain' (دارد is the present/habitual 'has', روان = soul/spirit).
segments.s314-g189: 'همه' quantifies the enumeration that follows ('every/all the mountains and rivers and deserts and waters'); the nouns are simply listed with the coordinating 'و'.
segments.s314-g190: 'نبیند' is a classical narrative negative of 'دیدن' and functions here as 'he did not see' (negative past in the epic style). 'نشانی ز' = 'a sign/trace of', with ز as the poetic preposition 'of/from', and 'افراسیاب' is the name being referred to.
segments.s314-g191: Again 'همی گفت' = 'he kept saying'. 'کای' is a vocative 'O' (archaic/epic), followed by the titles/epithets 'داور دادگر' — 'judge' plus the adjective 'just' (literal order: noun + adjective).
segments.s314-g192: The clause 'تو دادی مرا نازش و زور و فر' = 'you gave me (مرا) his/that/its naaz, and strength, and farr (glory)'. 'تو دادی' is simple past 'you gave'; 'مرا' is the accusative 'me' (here shown split as م + را in the word list); the three nouns form a coordinated object. 'ناز' in this context reads best as 'pride/regard/cherishing' and 'ش' is the possessive clitic, while 'زور' = 'strength' and 'فر' = 'royal glory'.
segments.s314-g17: بجستند is past tense, 3rd-person plural (they sought/searched). The prepositional phrase بر دشت و باغ و سرای lists the locations searched; بر here has the usual poetic sense ‘in/over/through’ and the coordinated nouns are linked with و. Word order is verb first (poetic), then the locations as its objects.
segments.s314-g18: گرفتند is past 3rd-plural ‘they took/seized’. بر هر سوی رهنمای is a phrase meaning ‘(they) took guides toward/on every side’: بر has a directional/’toward’ force, هر سوی = ‘every direction/side’, and رهنمای is the object ‘guide(s)’. The clause follows the previous one as a coordinated action.
segments.s314-g19: همی is a frequentative/continuative adverb (still/continually) that with رفت gives an iterative past ‘kept going’. جوینده is the agent ‘the seeker’ placed after the verb (poetic inversion). چون بیهشان = ‘as if without them’; بیه is an archaic preposition ‘without’ combined with the clitic -شان.
segments.s314-g20: مگر here introduces a proviso/condition ‘unless/except’ (often in the sense ‘until’/‘hoping that’). ز و represents archaic از + او ‘from him’ (z = from; the following و is the clitic/pronoun spelling here). بیابند is a subjunctive/optative form ‘they may find’ (expressing possibility), and جایی نشان = ‘some sign of a place’ is its object.
segments.s314-g21: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when’. بر جستنش = ‘in/for the search of him’ (بر + جستن with the pronominal suffix -ش = ‘for him’). تیز بشتافتند is the main clause ‘they rushed/hurried quickly’ (تیز = swift; بشتافتند = they hurried). The order places the temporal clause first, then the hurried action.
segments.s314-g22: فراوان here quantifies the following partitive phrase: ز کسهای او یافتند = ‘they found (lit. from) many of his people’. ز introduces a partitive/’among’ relation, کسهای او = ‘his people/men’, and یافتند is past plural ‘they found’. The overall sense: as they hurried to search, they came upon many of his followers.
segments.s314-g23: بکشتند is past 3rd-plural ‘they killed’. بسیار کس = ‘many people’ (بسیار here = ‘many’), and بی‌گناه is an adjective ‘innocent’ modifying کس. Word order: verb, then the quantified object phrase.
segments.s314-g24: نشانی نیامد is an impersonal negated clause ‘no sign came’ or ‘there was no trace’. ز introduces the source ‘from’ (ز بیداد شاه = ‘from the king’s injustice/tyranny’), so the full meaning is that the king’s oppression produced no sign (i.e. showed no mitigation or sign of restraint). نیامد is simple past negative ‘did not come’.
segments.s314-g57: بدانگه is an initial adverbial meaning "at that moment"; که introduces a subordinate clause (here temporal "when"); بیدار is a predicative adjective "awake" and گیرد/گردد (گـِردَدن) is a literary third-person singular present/subjunctive meaning "becomes" or "is come to be". The clause follows a somewhat inverted, poetic order: predicate + verb + subject (بیدار گردد خروس = "the rooster becomes awake").
segments.s314-g58: ز درگاه is a prepositional/locative phrase "from the gate/portal" (ز is the poetic form of از). برخاست is the past (or pastive) verb "rose/was raised". آوای کوس is a nominal phrase "the sound/voice of the drum" (آوا + کوس in genitive relation); in poetry the genitive link is compacted without ezāfe. The overall order is typical of Ferdowsi's verse: locative phrase + verb + nominal object.
segments.s314-g59: سپاهی is used as a collective noun "army/host"; شتابنده is a following adjectival participle "hastening" (adjective form modifying سپاهی). و simply coordinates the next noun. راه‌جوی is a compound noun "path-seeker/scout". The whole phrase is a coordinated nominal subject phrase that will govern the verb نهادند.
segments.s314-g60: به سوی is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning "toward(s)"; بیابان is the destination "the desert/wilderness". نهادند is the past plural of نهادن (here "they set/placed") used in the idiom نهادنِ روی = "to set/turn one's face"; روی ("face") completes that idiom, so نهادند روی = "they turned their faces / they headed" toward the desert.
segments.s314-g61: همه نامداران هر کشوری is a noun phrase meaning "all the renowned men of every country/realm": همه (all) quantifies نامداران (famous men), and هر کشوری is a genitive/attributive phrase "of each country" specifying whose men they are.
segments.s314-g62: برفتند is the simple past plural "they went/left." هر جا که is a standard relative/conditional "wherever/whenever that." بد مهتری is a compressed nominal phrase in which بد ("bad") qualifies مهتری (here "chief/preeminence/leadership"); together it specifies the places: "where there was bad leadership." Grammatically, بد مهتری is the predicate of the clause introduced by هر جا که.
segments.s314-g63: خورشها is the topicalized plural object "the stews/dishes." ببردند is a past-tense form meaning "they carried/brought" (بـ + بردند, literary). نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase "near the king" indicating where the dishes were placed. Word order places the object first for emphasis, then the verb and the locative.
segments.s314-g64: که introduces a relative clause describing شاه. بود is the past form "was." از در شهریار و سپاه is a poetic locative phrase: از در (literally "from the gate/threshold") here functions as "at/within the royal presence/portal," and شهریار و سپاه names who is present there (the sovereign and the army). Together the clause qualifies the king's position "who was at the sovereign's gate with the host."
segments.s314-g49: تنش را به خلعت بیاراستند is a past-tense clause meaning 'they adorned his body with a robe.' تن (body) takes the possessive clitic ش (his) immediately after it, and را is the definite object marker placed after the noun phrase. به + خلعت marks the instrument/thing used ('with a robe of honour'), and بیاراستند is a third-person plural past verb ('they adorned'). Word order is Subject (implied) – Object (تنش را) – Prepositional phrase (به خلعت) – Verb (بیاراستند).
segments.s314-g50: ز دژ باره مرزبان خواستند is another past-tense clause: ز دژ ('from the fortress') sets the origin; باره here is a noun following the prepositional phrase (classical usages vary), and مرزبان is the person referred to (the marzban). خواستند is the third-person plural past of خواستن and here functions as 'they requested/summoned/called for.' The verb agrees with a plural subject (they) and the prepositional phrase precedes the verb, as is common in poetic word order.
segments.s314-g51: چنین گفت کایدر به شادی بمان: چنین گفت is a formulaic poetic sequence meaning 'thus he said.' کایدر here functions as a vocative/name addressed directly (O Kaydar). به شادی بمان is an imperative/hortative expression ('remain in joy' or 'stay joyful'), where به شادی ('in/with joy') precedes the verb بمان ('remain/stay'). The order is typical: formulaic remark + vocative + instruction.
segments.s314-g52: ز دل بر کن اندیشه بدگمان is an imperative clause meaning 'put away from the heart the suspicious thought.' ز دل ('from the heart') gives the source; بر کن is the compound imperative (بر + کن) meaning 'do off / remove / cast off' (بر here is directional/away + کن imperative of کردن); اندیشه بدگمان is the direct object ('the suspicious thought'), adjective following the noun as usual in Persian. The overall command is 'remove suspicious thought from your heart.'
segments.s314-g53: ببخشید چندان که بد خواسته: ببخشید here is past-tense plural of بخشیدن but in context means 'they bestowed/gave.' چندان که is a quantitative connector 'so much that' (چندان = 'to that extent' + که = 'that/so that' linking clause). بد خواسته functions as a compound meaning 'one who wished ill' or 'ill-wisher' (literally 'bad-wished'), the logical patient/affected party of the extent clause. The phrase sets up a result clause introduced by که.
segments.s314-g54: ز اسبان وز گنج آراسته: this is a short result clause describing how (or with what) someone was adorned. ز اسبان = 'with horses' (z = 'with/from' in poetic usage), وز گنج is a coordination meaning 'and with treasure' (classical texts sometimes use وز = و + ز or a fused conjunctive form), and آراسته is a predicate adjective 'adorned / decked out.' The clause thus says the (ill-wisher) was decked out with horses and treasure.
segments.s314-g55: همه شهر ز ایشان توانگر شدند is a past-tense result clause: همه شهر ('the whole city') is the subject, ز ایشان ('because of them' or 'through them') gives cause, and توانگر شدند ('became wealthy') is the verb phrase with plural agreement. The structure is Cause (ز ایشان) embedded between subject and predicate, typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s314-g56: چه با یاره و تخت و افسر شدند is an explanatory continuation: چه here introduces an explanation ('for / because'), با marks accompaniment or possession ('with'), and the coordinated list یاره و تخت و افسر names what they had (attendants, a throne, and helmets). شدند is the past plural verb ('they were / became'), echoing the previous verb to show the means by which the city prospered. Word order places the explanatory 'for' clause after the main statement, a common classical device.
segments.s314-g33: که‌گر is an archaic fused conditional ('if'); شاه را دل is an inverted possessive/topic structure where را marks 'شاه' (the king) as the topic and دل (literally 'heart') is the possessed idea — together 'the king's heart/mind'. نجنبد is the negative present/ subjunctive/indicative form of جنبیدن ('to move, be roused') meaning 'does not stir' and ز جای is the poetic prepositional phrase ('from his place/seat') using ز for 'از'.
segments.s314-g34: سوی شهر ایران is a directional prepositional phrase ('toward the city of Iran'). نیایدش is the negative present/optative/impersonal form of نیامدن ('not come') with the enclitic -ش ('to him/it') attached; رای ('counsel, plan') is the noun being negated — 'no counsel will come to it/him' (here: to the city/realm). Word order places the adverbial phrase before the verb plus enclitic.
segments.s314-g35: همانا is an emphatic particle ('verily/indeed') placed before the clause. بداندیش is an adjective/noun meaning 'ill‑minded, malicious' modifying the proper name افراسیاب, which is the subject here: 'the malicious Afrasiab.' The order is emphatic particle + epithet + name.
segments.s314-g36: گذشته‌ست is the perfect/past participial construction 'has passed/has gone' (classical Persian perfect). زان سو is a compact phrase 'from that side / on that side' using زان ('from that') + سو ('side/toward'). به دریای آب is the directional complement 'to the sea' (دریای 'sea' governed by the preposition به 'toward/into' and آب 'water' used poetically).
segments.s314-g37: چنان is an adverb meaning 'so/thus' modifying پیر ('old one' — here describing someone as aged). بر گاه is a classical locative 'upon/at the court' (بر + گاه), and کاووس شاه is the name with title following (Kavus the king). The phrase orders degree/adverb + noun + locative + possessor/title: 'so old at the court of King Kavus.'
segments.s314-g38: The list introduced by نه ... و ... و نه ... is a classical negative coordination: نه introduces items lacking ('neither ...'), و is the coordinating 'and', and the second نه repeats the negation for the second pair. The nouns enumerate things absent: اورنگ (throne), فر (royal glory), گنج (treasure), سپاه (army). The construction implies 'there is neither X nor Y.'
segments.s314-g39: گر is the conditional 'if'. او is the third‑person pronoun ('he'). سوی ایران شود is a clause meaning 'becomes/turns toward Iran' where شود is the present/subjunctive/auxiliary of شدن ('become/go'), and سوی ایران gives the direction. پر ز کین is a predicative phrase 'full of (پر) hatred/vengeance (کین)', using ز as the poetic 'of'/'from' marker: 'full of hatred.' Together: 'If he comes toward Iran full of hatred...'.
segments.s314-g40: که here introduces the interrogative/relative clause 'who/that' ('who will be...?'). باشد is the subjunctive/potential form of 'to be' (here 'will be / can be'), and نگهبان ایران‌زمین is the predicate noun phrase 'the guardian of the land of Iran' (نگهبان 'guardian', ایران‌زمین 'Iranian realm'). The clause therefore asks rhetorically 'who will be the guardian of Iran?'
segments.s314-g9: بدید is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'he/it saw' placed at the start for poetic word order. آن دل‌افروز باغ بهشت is a noun phrase 'that heart-delighting garden of Paradise' where آن (that) points to the garden and دل‌افروز is a compound adjective modifying باغ; بهشت is a genitival/attributive noun ('of Paradise').
segments.s314-g10: شمرهای او is a possessive plural noun phrase 'his Shams / his cruel ones' (شمر in plural), where او is the possessor. چون here is a simile marker 'like/as', followed by چراغ بهشت 'lamp of Paradise' (چراغ = lamp, بهشت = Paradise) forming the comparison.
segments.s314-g11: به هر گوشه‌ای is a locative phrase 'in/at every corner' with به used in a locative/towards sense and گوشه‌ای = 'a corner/spot'. چشمه و گلستان is a simple coordinate noun phrase 'springs and rose-gardens' (و = and). This is a descriptive listing of features found in each corner.
segments.s314-g12: زمین here means 'the ground/earth' and starts the clause describing surface-covering vegetation: سنبل 'hyacinth' coordinated (و) with شاخ بلبلستان 'the boughs/branches of the land of nightingales' (شاخ = branch; بلبلستان = poetic place 'land of nightingales'). Word order is noun + enumerated modifiers.
segments.s314-g13: همی گفت is a habitual/continuous marker (همی) plus the verb 'said' (گفت), giving 'kept saying' or 'would say'. هر کس که اینت نهاد is a relative clause 'whoever/each person who placed this'—اینت is a demonstrative/dialectal 'this' and نهاد here is the past of نهادن 'to place/put' (i.e., 'had placed').
segments.s314-g14: هم ایدر بباشیم is a hortative/optative clause: هم 'also/indeed', ایدر 'here', بباشیم (subjunctive/hortative of بودن) 'let us remain / may we be'. تا مرگ شاد is a temporal/teleological phrase 'until death (and) happy' where تا = 'until' and مرگ شاد = 'happy in death' / 'happy until death.'
segments.s314-g15: وز آن پس is a sequence-of-time phrase 'and/from that then/after that' (پس = after). بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he ordered/commanded/said', and بیدار شاه is an address or brief imperative label 'awake, O king' (بیدار = awake; شاه = king). Note: وز in the given senses list is not the usual prepositional use here; the phrase functions as a temporal connective.
segments.s314-g16: طلب کردن is a compound verb 'to seek/look for' where طلب is the noun/root 'seeking' and کردن functions as the light/auxiliary verb. The following words شاه توران سپاه specify the object: 'the king (شاه) of Turan's army' or more naturally 'the Turanian army' (توران = Turan, سپاه = army). Word order places the verbal compound before its object phrase.
segments.s314-g105: پیاده شد is a light-predicate past: literally 'became on-foot' = 'dismounted' (شد as past auxiliary/verb). از اسب is a prepositional phrase 'from the horse' marking source. The coordinating و then links this clause to the following action. روی زمین is an adverbial phrase 'on the ground' with روی meaning 'upon'.
segments.s314-g106: ببوسید is simple past 'he kissed' with the object (the ground) understood from the previous phrase. و links to the next clause. بر شاه is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to the king' (بر = toward/to), and کرد آفرین uses the light verb کرد plus the noun/exclamation آفرین to mean 'he gave/poured out praise' or 'he cried “bravo”'.
segments.s314-g107: همه is the subject 'everyone'. The coordinated adjectives/nominals تیز و مکران describe the parties being arrayed (تیز here as 'swift/keen', مکران as the toponym 'Makran' used adjectivally or as a group name). بیاراستند is past plural 'they arrayed/adorned' or 'they made ready'. The verb at the end is the finite past agreeing with همه.
segments.s314-g108: ز هر جای is a prepositional phrase 'from every place/quarter' (ز = from, هر = every, جای = place). رامشگران is the direct object 'peacemakers' or mediators; خواستند is simple past 'they requested/summoned' (third-person plural). The clause therefore means they summoned such people from everywhere.
segments.s314-g109: همه راه و بی‌راه is an idiomatic pair 'on every road and off-road' = 'everywhere' (راه = road/way, بی‌راه used adverbially for 'off the road' or 'wrong/unwarranted place'). آوای رود is a genitive phrase 'the sound/voice (آوا) of the رود' — here رود is the musical instrument (the lute/strings), so 'the lute's song' or 'the sound of the strings'.
segments.s314-g110: تو گفتی is 'you said' (گفتی is past 2nd-singular); the provided lexical senses for گفتی did not include 'said' so that item is marked -1 below. هوا تار شد is a poetic copular clause: هوا (the air/atmosphere) is subject and تار شد 'became the warp' (تار here = warp in weaving imagery). The final رشْتِرانه pairing رود پود places رود ('strings' or lute) with پود ('weft'), an elliptical verbless or paratactic clause implying 'and the strings became the weft' — a compact, poetic image rather than a strict syntactic sentence.
segments.s314-g111: به دیوار دیبا is a goal phrase 'upon the wall, brocade' (به = toward/onto, دیوار = wall, دیبا = brocade). برآویختند is a past plural verb meaning 'they hung up / suspended' (a classical/poetic verb). The object (دیبا) appears before the verb, which is normal in Persian prose/poetry.
segments.s314-g112: درم با شکر lists two things 'dirhams and sugar' with با linking 'with' but functioning like 'and'. زیر پی is a compound adverbial 'under the feet' (زیر = under, پی = foot/step) and ریختند is past plural 'they poured/scattered/sprinkled'. Together the clause means they cast coins and sugar beneath the feet (i.e. scattered on the ground).
segments.s314-g169: به marks the indirect object ('to Gostaham'). گستهم is the proper name Gostaham (the provided sense list does not include the name). فرمود is the 3rd-person past of فرمودن 'to say/command' (here 'he ordered/said'). تا introduces a reported command or purpose clause ('that / so that'), and برنشست (بر-نشست) is a past action 'took a seat / sat down' used here as the content of that order.
segments.s314-g170: همه راه is a phrase meaning 'the whole way / all the way' (همه = whole/entire modifying راه). شادان is an adjective 'joyful, rejoicing' describing the manner of the journey. و links the next clause; دستش is دست + enclitic -ش 'his hand', and به دست literally 'to/into hand' here idiomatically 'hand in hand' (preposition به introduces the linkage between hands).
segments.s314-g171: کشیدند is 3rd-person plural past of کشیدن 'they moved/drew/marched'. زان روی is an idiomatic conjunctive phrase: زان ('for that reason') + روی ('on/upon', here part of the idiom) giving 'thereupon/for that reason'. به + بهشت is a prepositional phrase 'to/into paradise' and گنگ ('mute/quiet, hushed') is a postpositive adjective qualifying بهشت.
segments.s314-g172: سپه را: سپه 'army/host' with the object marker را (definite object). به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the vicinity/near to'. شاه is the head noun 'king' and آب و رنگ is a descriptive phrase ('literally water and color') used idiomatically for appearance/complexion/brightness — here an epithet describing the king's appearance. The whole string means they brought the army up close to the king, who is described by آب و رنگ.
segments.s314-g173: وفا is an abstract noun 'fidelity/loyalty'. چون is a simile marker 'like/as'. درختی is 'a tree' (here the metaphor's noun). بود is the past copula 'was', linking the subject to the predicate adjective میوه‌دار 'fruit-bearing / fruitful'.
segments.s314-g174: همی is a frequentative/emphatic adverb 'always/still', and هر زمانی literally 'every time / at every season' is an adverbial phrase; نو آید is present/habitual 'a new (one) comes' (آید used here in a timeless/habitual sense). به بار is part of the idiom به بار آمدن 'to bear fruit / to yield' — بار is the 'load/produce' that the tree bears.
segments.s314-g175: نیاسود is a past negative perfective 'did not rest / found no repose' (literally 'did not repose'). یک تن is 'one person' or 'a single soul' (تن used as 'person'). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (ز = از), governing خورد و شکار where خورد is used as a noun/gerund 'eating/food' and شکار 'the hunt/prey'; together 'from food and the hunt'. The clause says no person took rest from dining and hunting.
segments.s314-g176: همان repeated here is a demonstrative intensifier 'that very / the same'. یک سواره 'one rider' identifies a single mounted man; the second همان reiterates and introduces شهریار 'the king' — i.e., 'that one rider, that king' (an emphatic identification). Word order is emphatic and typical of epic diction.
segments.s314-g209: "همی بود" is a literary past-progressive: همی before the verb gives a continued or ongoing past sense ("was remaining"); here بود is the past copula. یک سال is a temporal duration complement placed after the verb. در + noun is the locative phrase (در حصن گنگ = "in the fortress Gung" or "in the silent fortress"); حصن is the head noun and گنگ qualifies it as a name/descriptor.
segments.s314-g210: برآسود is a perfective/past adjectival verb (from برآسیدن) meaning 'was at rest' or 'rested'; it governs the following "از"-phrase. از introduces the source/thing he is free of or relieved from. جنبش و ساز جنگ is a coordinated noun phrase: جنبش (agitation/movement) and ساز جنگ (ساز used as 'means/preparations' of جنگ = 'war').
segments.s314-g211: چو here introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when'. بودن is used as a verbal noun ('the being/staying'); the locative phrase به گنگ اندرون ('into/within Gung') follows, and شد (become) takes دراز ('long') as its predicative complement—literally 'when the being in Gung became long'.
segments.s314-g212: به دیدار marks the purpose/direction 'to (a) meeting/visit'; کاووسش is the proper name Kavus with the third-person possessive suffix -ش ('his Kavus' — typical in poetry to indicate association). آمد is the past 'came' and نیاز functions as a noun meaning 'supplication/entreaty' (i.e. he came to plead). Word order places the goal phrase before the verb and the manner/intent (نیاز) after the verb.
segments.s314-g213: سپرد is the past transitive 'entrusted/handed over'; when a recipient is expressed it is introduced by به (به گستهم = 'to Gostaham'). آن زمین is the direct object 'that land' placed after the verb (poetic order). نوذر here is a name in apposition/adjacent to گستهم (a proper-name unit in the text).
segments.s314-g214: ز ... تا marks a geographic span: ز قچغار ('from Quchghar') تا پیش دریای چین ('up to/in front of the Sea of China'). ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from'; پیش here means 'before/in front of' and دریای چین is a genitive construction 'the sea of China' (China = place name).
segments.s314-g215: بی‌اندازه is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'immeasurable/excessive' modifying لشکر (army), so the sequence reads 'an immense army'. The recipient is marked by به + name (به گستهم = 'to Gostaham'), and داد is the past 'gave' (transitive), completing the action.
segments.s314-g216: بدو is a fused prepositional pronoun (به + او = 'to him') meaning 'to him'; گفت is the past 'said' (here used as a speech verb). بیدار دل باش و شاد is a compound imperative: بیدار (awake/alert) modifies دل ('heart') and باش is the imperative 'be'; و links the second imperative/adjective شاد ('joyful'). The phrase is an addressed command: 'Be alert-hearted and glad.'
segments.s314-g241: This clause is a sequence of simple past narrative actions. بخورد (be+خورد) is a classical/perhaps archaic past-form meaning “he ate” and begins the series; و links items in the series. بیاسود here functions as a past participial/adjectival-predicate meaning “reposed/was at rest” (an older form built with بی-). و links to the following temporal phrase یک هفته بود, where بود is the past-tense copula meaning “was/there was” and هفته is the time-span “week” placed after the verb as is normal in Persian narrative (‘‘he was a week’’ = “he stayed a week”).
segments.s314-g242: دوم هفته is a continuing temporal phrase “the second week.” با introduces the manner/condition “with” and جامه is the noun “garment(s).” نابسود is an older evaluative adjective describing the state of the garments or the person’s satisfaction; here it qualifies the situation in that second week (rendered in English as “unsatisfied”/‘‘unrelieved’’). The clause omits an explicit copula which is understood from the previous بود.
segments.s314-g243: بیامد is the past tense “came” (simple narrative). خروشان is an adverbial/adjectival participle meaning “roaring/tumultuous” and modifies the manner of coming. به + آتشکده marks direction/goal (“to the fire‑temple”), with به placed before the destination noun as usual.
segments.s314-g244: This clause states a resulting condition: غمی بود, literally “there was sorrow” (غمی = grief as subject, بود = past copula). زآن introduces the cause/reasonal phrase “from that / because,” and اژدهای شده is a verbal-adjectival phrase meaning “having become a dragon” or “a dragon having been formed” (شده is the perfect/passive participle ‘become/been made’ modifying اژدهای).
segments.s314-g245: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “which/that.” تور is the proper name Tūr (not the common noun ‘net’ here) and فریدون qualifies him (Tūr of Fereydun). برآورده بود is a compound past construction: برآورده (a past participle/verb meaning ‘raised/produced/provided’) plus بود as the past copula, yielding “had raised” or “was raised” depending on voice — here it means Tūr had been (earlier) established/raised.
segments.s314-g246: بدو = به + او (to it/him) meaning “for it/for him”; اندرون کاخها is the object phrase “inner palaces” (اندرون = inside/inner, کاخها = palaces). کرده بود is the past perfect construction (کرده, past participle ‘done/made’, plus بود) meaning “had made” or “had built.” Together: “(he) had made inner palaces for it/him.”
segments.s314-g247: بگسترد is a past-tense verb ‘he spread/distributed’ (classical narrative form). بر + موبدان marks the indirect object/recipients ‘to the mōbeds (priests).’ سیم و زر is a coordinated noun phrase ‘silver and gold’ (coordinated by و) serving as the theme of distribution.
segments.s314-g248: بر آتش indicates the location/target ‘upon the fire.’ پراگند is a past/poetic form meaning ‘scattered’ or ‘threw about.’ چندی گهَر = ‘some jewels’ (چندی = some/several, گهر = jewel) — together: ‘he scattered several jewels on the fire.’ Word order places the target (بر آتش) before the verb for emphasis, a common poetic choice.
segments.s314-g121: ورا is the object pronoun 'to him' (enclitic in prose but here separate), followed by کرد 'made/did'. مهتر is the predicate noun 'chief/headman' and به + مکران زمین marks the domain ('over the land of Makran'). The line order is poetic but reads: 'he made him (ورا کرد) chief (مهتر) of the land of Makran (به مکرانِ زمین)'.
segments.s314-g122: بسی here functions adverbially as 'much/many', modifying خلعتش داد 'gave him robes (of honour)'; خلعتش is 'robe(s) of honour' with the possessive -ش 'his/him'. The coordinate و کرد آفرین is a short clause 'and he made (said) "afarin"' — a formulaic praise/acclamation (کرد آفرین = 'praised/commended').
segments.s314-g123: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when he came'. ز marks source 'from Makran and Turan' (ز مکران و توران). به چین is the destination; چین here means 'array/camp/ranks' (poetic use), so 'came to his array/camp'.
segments.s314-g124: خود is a possessive/emphatic 'his/own' qualifying چین (his camp/array). The coordinate و سرفرازان ایران‌زمین adds 'and the proud (sarfrazan) ones of the Iranian land' — a coordinated noun phrase apposed to or conjoined with 'his camp'.
segments.s314-g125: پذیره شدش is a passive/consequence construction 'he was received' or 'reception befell him', with the enclitic -ش marking the person affected. The names رستم زال سام follow as the agents/hosts: 'by Rostam, Zal, and Sam' (classical Persian often places agents after the verb in narration).
segments.s314-g126: سپاهی here denotes the group of warriors/army; the adjectives گشاده دل ('open‑hearted', idiomatic compound) and شاد کام ('joyful/with fulfilled desire') follow the noun as attributive descriptions. The phrase describes the temper of the warriors who received him.
segments.s314-g127: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. از دور 'from afar' gives origin/distance, کیخسرو is the subject 'Kay Khosrow', and آمد پدید is a poetic verb pair meaning 'came into view/appeared' (پدید used with آمد to indicate emergence/appearance).
segments.s314-g128: سوار سرفراز = 'a proud/victorious rider/horseman' (adjective after noun). چترش is 'his canopy/cover' (چتر + possessive -ش). کشید is the past of کشیدن 'to draw/drag/raise' — here 'unfurled/raised/drew' his canopy; the verb stands at the end as usual in Persian poetry.
segments.s314-g233: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. دمدار is a poetical epithet placed after چو; here it functions as a time/scene noun ('at dawn' in sense) and is linked to the verb برداشتی. برداشتی is a past action 'took/took up' (verb) and in older poetic Persian the verb can appear before or after its subject; پیشرو ('vanguard/forerunner') is the actor/theme associated with that verb.
segments.s314-g234: به منزل رسیدی is a prepositional phrase ('reached/arrived at the lodging') with رسیدی as the past verb 'arrived/reached'. همی is the archaic/poetic continuative particle indicating ongoing or habitual aspect ('still/continually') and it scopes over the adverbial phrase نو به نو ('new to new' = 'anew, repeatedly'), where the middle به is the directional/relational preposition linking the two نو elements.
segments.s314-g235: بیامد is the past intransitive verb 'came/appeared'. بر آن نشان is a prepositional phrase 'upon that a sign' (بر + آن = 'on/over that', نشان = 'sign/token'), and هم is a simple emphatic/also marking that the sign was an additional thing. تا introduces the following clause and here has a temporal/continuative sense 'until' or 'up to'. به چاج is a prepositional phrase indicating the goal/place 'to/at Chāj' (Chāj is a proper name/place).
segments.s314-g236: بیاویخت is a past transitive verb 'he hung/affixed' (an older poetic form equivalent to آویخت/آویخت). تاج is the direct object 'the crown'. از بر تخت عاج is a compound locative/relational phrase: از بر in older Persian often marks position 'on/over', تخت = 'throne', عاج = 'ivory' (an adjective or attributive noun), so the whole phrase indicates the crown being hung upon the ivory throne. The verb precedes its object and locative phrase here as is common in poetic word order.
segments.s314-g237: به سغد اندرون بود is a locative clause: به سغد ('into/to Sogd') + اندرون ('inside/within') followed by the past copula بود ('was'). یک هفته is a temporal phrase 'one week' specifying duration and appears before the noun شاه which here functions as the subject in this inverted, poetic order (so: 'the king was inside Sogd for one week').
segments.s314-g238: همه سغد شد is a nominal phrase where همه سغد ('all Sogd' — the people of Sogd) is the subject and شد is the copular/verb 'became'. شاه را نیک‌خواه is a small clause with را marking شاه as the beneficiary/target and نیک‌خواه meaning 'well-wisher' — together 'became well-wishers toward the king'. The object marker را attaches to شاه to show the direction/target of their goodwill.
segments.s314-g239: وز آنجا به شهر بخارا رسید starts with an adverbial/location phrase; وز is an archaic/poetic prepositional/relational element meaning 'from/whence' (here introducing the origin 'from there'), آنجا = 'there', followed by the motion phrase به شهر بخارا رسید ('he arrived at the city of Bukhara') where رسید is the past verb 'arrived'.
segments.s314-g240: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the army' (ز = 'from' in poetic usage). هوا را is an accusative phrase where هوا ('air/mood/desire') is the object marked by را; here هوا most likely refers to a 'movement/manifestation' or 'mood' rather than literal air. همی is the continuative/intonation particle and کس ندید is the negated past verb phrase 'no one saw' (کس = 'anyone/no one' depending on negation, ندید = 'did not see'). Altogether: 'From the army, no one saw it/that movement.'
segments.s314-g1: پس is a discourse adverb meaning 'then/therefore' placed before the clause. آگاهی is a noun ('news/information') functioning as the subject of آمد ('came/arrived'); the verb آمد follows the noun as in narrative Persian. به + proper name (به افراسیاب) marks the recipient/target of that news.
segments.s314-g2: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the reported-clause. شاه جهاندار is a nominal phrase with جهاندار an epithet/appositive to شاه ('the sovereign/world-ruler'). بگذاشت is past tense (بـ- prefix + گذاشت) meaning 'he put/left/let' here used as the main verb of the reported clause, and آب (literally 'water') is its object; the clause means 'that the sovereign king left water' (the exact idiomatic sense of آب may be literal or figurative, see note).
segments.s314-g3: شنیده is a past participle ('what he had heard' / 'the heard [thing]') functioning as the matter being kept; همی داشت is a classical/periphrastic progressive ('kept / was keeping') where داشت + past-participle/verb expresses continuous or habitual action. اندر نهفت ('in concealment') completes the idea: 'kept (it) concealed.'
segments.s314-g4: بیامد (aorist/past narrative) = 'came' (here 'night came'); شب تیره is a noun phrase 'a dark night' where تیره modifies شب. با کس نگفت is literal 'he did not tell anyone': نگفت is the negated past form (ن‑ + گفت) 'did not say/tell' and با کس ('with anyone') expresses the indirect object/recipient (effectively 'to no one' because of the negation).
segments.s314-g5: جهاندیدگان is a plural nominal 'those who have seen the world' (experienced/worldly-wise people). را is the object/definiteness marker used here for emphasis, هم آنجا bماند renders 'also remained there' — بماند is a narrative-form verb meaning 'remained' (3rd-person), placed at the end of the clause as usual.
segments.s314-g6: This is a compact nominal phrase + verb: دلی پر ز تیمار = 'a heart full of care' (دلی: 'a heart', پر ز = 'full of', تیمار = 'care/solicitude'); تنها is an adverb 'alone/only', and براند (راند + ب‑) is the verb 'drives away' or 'drove away' placed at the end. The subject is omitted but understood from context; the clause says 'he alone drives/drave away a heart full of care.'
segments.s314-g7: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کیخسرو is the proper name (the subject). آمد is the past narrative 'came/entered'. به + اندرون ('to the inner [chamber]') marks the destination; گنگ modifies اندرون as 'silent/muted' (i.e. 'the silent inner room'). Word order: temporal subordinate (چو ...) + verb-final main clause.
segments.s314-g8: Two parallel nominal phrases describe his condition: سری پر ز تیمار = 'a head full of care' and دل پر ز خون = 'a heart full of blood'. سری and دل are nouns (head / heart), each followed by پر ز + noun to mean 'full of ...'. This is a verbless descriptive continuation referring back to the subject (Kay Khosrow).
segments.s314-g113: به مکران is a locative prepositional phrase placing the people in/with relation to Makran (here rendered 'from Makran' in context). هر آن کس که introduces a free relative ('anyone who/each person who') and the final adjectival phrase بد مهتری describes that person as of poor or ill repute in rank (بد modifying مهتری). Word order is inverted in poetic register: the location and the relative clause appear before the main verb, which is carried in the following line.
segments.s314-g114: وگر here works as a coordinating/conditional particle meaning 'or/and if' (used like 'whether' or 'even if') to present alternatives; نامداری and کنداوری are parallel nominal items giving two other kinds of persons (a notable/renowned man, and the proper name Kandauri). The construction continues the list of people who (as the relative clause implies) will be doing the action that follows in the next sentence.
segments.s314-g115: برفتند is a past tense, 3rd-person plural verb ('they went' / 'they departed'). The verb appears at the start here by poetic inversion; the complements با هدیه و با نثار ('with gift and with offering') are prepositional adjuncts specifying accompaniment/means. The repeated با adds rhythmic emphasis.
segments.s314-g116: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the vicinity / to the presence of'; پیروزگر شهریار is a nominal phrase where پیروزگر ('victorious/conqueror') qualifies شهریار ('king'). Together they specify the destination of برفتند ('to the victorious king').
segments.s314-g117: و زآن مرز begins 'and from that border/region' (زآن = 'from that'); چندان که functions like 'as many as' / 'to such an extent that' and introduces a restrictive clause; بد خواسته here is an adjectival phrase 'having bad intent' or 'ill-disposed' describing those people. The clause sets up which people (the ill-intentioned ones) perform the action that follows.
segments.s314-g118: فراز آورید is a past verb phrase 'they raised up / brought aloft' (فراز = 'on high / up', آورید = past 3pl 'brought/raised'); اشکش ('their tear(s)') is the object and آراسته ('adorned, artificial, made-up') qualifies that object. Poetic word-order places the object phrase after the verb here, but the sense is 'they produced/raised their embellished tears.'
segments.s314-g119: ز اشکش ('on account of/from their tear(s)') gives the cause or occasion; پذیرفت is the past tense 'the king accepted' (شاه is the grammatical subject placed after the verb for poetic inversion), and آنچه دید ('what he saw') is the object clause ('that which he perceived'). The line therefore means the king accepted (the gifts/offers) by/through the effect of their tears.
segments.s314-g120: و زآن introduces the next action 'and from those (same) notables'; نامداران is a plural noun 'the illustrious/renowned ones', یکی is 'one (of them)', and برگزید is the past tense 'he selected/chose'. The clause states the king chose one of those notable men.
segments.s314-g41: گر introduces a conditional ('if'). او is the subject 'he'. باز here is an adverb 'again'. با + nouns (تخت و افسر) marks being invested 'with throne and (diadem/helmet)'; افسر in classical / poetic usage stands for the crown/diadem. شود is the classical/present-subjunctive form of 'to become / to be restored' placed at the end of the clause.
segments.s314-g42: همه رنج ما is 'all our suffering' (همه = all; رنج = suffering; ما = our/us). پاک is used adverbially 'completely/utterly'. بی‌بر is an archaic/poetic predicate here implying bereft/void or wiped away (used idiomatically), and شود is again the copular/optative 'becomes / will be'.
segments.s314-g43: از آن پس forms a temporal phrase 'from that time / after that' (از = from; آن = that/then; پس = then/after). به ایرانیان is the indirect object 'to the Iranians'. شاه گفت is 'the king said' (شاه subject + گفت 'said'); گفت here is the speech verb.
segments.s314-g44: که introduces the content clause 'that...'. این پند = 'this advice / admonition'. با is 'with' or 'together with' linking پند to its quality. سودمندیست is a classical fused form meaning 'is beneficial/useful' (sودمندی + -ست). جفت here means 'paired / twofold' — i.e. the advice has a matching usefulness or is doubly useful.
segments.s314-g45: از آن شارستان پس = 'from that town, then' (از + آن + شارستان + پس giving a temporal/locative frame). مهان را بخواند = 'he summoned/called the guests' — را marks مهان as the definite/direct object and بخواند (here) means 'called/summoned' (classical poetic verb form).
segments.s314-g46: وز is an archaic prepositional/linking particle here (equivalent to 'و از' = 'and from / and by that'). آن رنج بردن = 'that suffering' expressed with the gerund/infinitival construction (رنج بردن = 'the enduring of pain' = 'suffering'). فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'much/abundantly'. براند is a present/subjunctive/epic form 'he drives away / removes' — so the clause means he drove away much of that suffering.
segments.s314-g47: از ایشان = 'from among them' (از = from; ایشان = them). کسی را is 'someone' with را marking the selected person as definite/object. که introduces a relative clause 'who is...'. شایسته‌تر is the comparative 'more worthy / more deserving' (شایسته + -تر).
segments.s314-g48: گرامی‌تر is the comparative 'more honored / dearer'. از introduces the standard comparative 'than'. شهر here is 'the city' (used as the comparison standard in the line). و links another comparative, and بایسته‌تر is 'more fitting / more proper' (comparative of بایسته). The phrase lists qualities of the chosen person using stacked comparatives.
segments.s314-g97: خورش کرد is a simple past clause: the noun خورش 'stew/dish' appears before the light verb کرد (past of کردن) — a common Persian construction meaning 'made/served a stew'. پوشش هم آنجا یله is a nominal clause where پوشش 'cloak/covering' is the topic, هم 'also/likewise' emphasizes it, آنجا 'there' is a locative, and یله 'loose/slack' is a predicative adjective placed after the locative phrase (typical poetic word order: topic — location — predicate).
segments.s314-g98: به governs an indirect object here ('to/for'); ملاح is in that dative meaning 'the sailor/helmsman'. و links a second indirect object phrase: آن کس که کردی خله is a noun plus a relative clause introduced by که. کردی is 2nd-person past 'you made', and خله is a predicate noun 'confidant/intimate' — the relative clause modifies آن کس ('that person whom you made a confidant').
segments.s314-g99: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb (classical register) meaning 'he commanded/ordered' (here with the sense 'gave/ordered to give'). It governs the direct objects دینار و خلعت ('dinar(s) and a robe of honour'), followed by the source phrase ز گنج 'from the treasury' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from').
segments.s314-g100: This is a beneficiary/recipient phrase: ز گیتی کسی را — 'to anyone in the world' — where ز گیتی ('of/from the world') qualifies the generic کسی 'anyone'; را is the definite/object marker attached to کسی to mark it as the recipient. The relative clause که بردند رنج ('who bore/endured hardship') modifies کسی: که introduces the clause and بردند is past plural 'they bore/took' used idiomatically for 'had endured.' رنج is 'suffering/hardship.'
segments.s314-g101: وز آن آب راه بیابان گرفت is compact poetic word order. وز here functions as a fused conjunctive/prepositional element ('and/from' in classical usage) linking what follows; آن آب 'that water' is the reference, راه بیابان 'the road to the desert' is the object/route, and گرفت (past of گرفتن) means 'took/seized' — i.e. 'he took the road into the desert from that water' or 'by/after that water he set out for the desert.' Word order (prepositional/topic before verb) is typical of epic verse.
segments.s314-g102: جهانی از او مانده اندر شگفت is an impersonal evaluative clause: جهانی 'the whole world' is the subject, از او 'about/at him' indicates the target of wonder, مانده (past participle/verb 'remained/was left') describes the resultant state, and اندر شگفت is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in astonishment/wonder.' Together: 'the whole world remained in astonishment at him.'
segments.s314-g103: چو آگاه شد is a temporal clause: چو 'when' + آگاه شد 'became aware' (شد is past of شدن used as copula/auxiliary). اشکش آمد به راه is idiomatic: اشکش 'his/her tear(s)' + آمد (past 'came') + به راه 'on the road/along the way' = 'his tears came upon the road', i.e. 'he began to weep as he went' or 'burst into tears on the road.' Word order puts the tear-subject before the motion verb, a poetic arrangement.
segments.s314-g104: ابا here functions as a proper name (not the verbal sense 'to refuse'); لشکری ساخته is a compound participial phrase: لشکری (troops/army) + ساخته (past participle 'made/formed') = 'having formed/mustered an army'. پیش شاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the king.' The overall reading: 'Aba, having mustered an army, stood/advanced before the king.'
segments.s314-g201: This clause is locative-fronted: به گیتی ('in the world') sets the scene, then از او ('of/from him') qualifies the following nouns. نام و آواز نیست is a verbless existential statement (نیست = 'does not exist/there is none') meaning 'there is no name and no fame/voice' relating back to 'of him'. 'از' here functions like the English possessive 'of' (archaic/poetic ordering: 'of him name and fame is not').
segments.s314-g202: ز من راز باشد uses ز ('from/of' in poetic register) + من ('me') with باشد in an optative/subjunctive sense ('let it be' / 'may there be')—i.e. 'let a secret be with me'. The second half, ز تو راز نیست, contrasts by denying that there is a secret with you (ز تو = 'as for you' / 'from you'). The structure is parallel and deliberately antithetical.
segments.s314-g203: The line begins with the conditional اگر ('if'). زو here is the poetic contraction of ز + او ('because of / concerning him'); word order is archaic/compact. تو is the subject 'you', خشنودی‌ای ('some approval/pleasure') is the predicative idea 'you are pleased', and دادگر is a vocative epithet 'O just one' (addressing a righteous/judging figure). The sequence reads as a conditional address: 'If you (are) pleased about him, O just one...'.
segments.s314-g204: مرا را is split here into two tokens (م + را) and together mark the definite object 'me'. بازگردان is an imperative 'return/restore (me)'. ز pِیکار سر is a short prepositional phrase 'from the battle, (my) head'—سر ('head') is used literally/metaphorically (i.e. life/head) so the sense is 'bring me back from battle, spare my head'. The verb is imperative and the phrase specifies the source/scene from which the speaker asks to be restored.
segments.s314-g205: بکش is an imperative verb here meaning 'drive/put (into) / draw' (not necessarily 'kill'—context shows sense of planting or forcing into). در دل ('in the heart') is the locative phrase, followed by این آتش کین من ('this fire of my hatred') as the thing to be driven in. کین modifies من (possessive) so the phrase is 'this fire of my vengeance of mine'.
segments.s314-g206: The structure is dyadic and somewhat chiasmic: به آیین خویش آور آیین من literally 'bring my custom into his custom'—به marks direction ('into/to'), آیین خویش = 'his/own ritual/practice' (خوی + ش shows the possessive), آور is the imperative 'bring', and آیین من is the object 'my ritual'. The sense is 'make my ritual conform to his way' (repetition of آیین emphasizes rites/customs).
segments.s314-g207: ز جای نیایش is a prepositional phrase 'from the place of prayer' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). بیامد is the past tense 'he came', and به تخت marks motion 'to the throne'. The clause is a simple past narrative sentence with the locative/source phrase fronted for emphasis.
segments.s314-g208: This is a nominal phrase describing the newcomer: جوان ('young' / 'young man') followed by two coordinating adjectives سرافراز ('lofty/triumphant') and پیروز ('victorious'), and the noun بخت ('fortune/fate') attached to qualify the victory—together 'a young, proud and victory-blessed (fortunate) man'. Adjectives follow the noun in the classical order.
segments.s314-g81: دو هفته is a pre-verbal duration phrase 'two weeks'. بر آن روی دریا is a prepositional/adverbial phrase: بر (on/upon) + آن (that) + روی دریا (the surface/course of the sea). بماند is the narrative verb meaning 'remained/stayed' (third person, subject omitted and supplied from context).
segments.s314-g82: ز گفتار با گیو = 'from/at the words (advice) of Giv' (ز is poetic 'از' meaning 'from/because of', گفتار 'words/speech', با 'with'). چندی براند: چندی 'for a while', براند (راند) 'drove / drove on' (past-narrative third person). Together: 'at Giv's words he/they drove on for a while.'
segments.s314-g83: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (standard narrative formula). هر کو ندیده‌ست = 'whoever has not seen' (هر کو is a relative 'whoever'; ندیده‌ست is perfect/present perfect 'has not seen'). گنگ is a predicate adjective 'mute/dumb' referring back to such a person; the whole phrase sketches the speaker's ignorance.
segments.s314-g84: نباید که = impersonal modal 'it should not be that' / 'one ought not'. خواهد is third-person present/future 'he/one wishes or will', forming 'it should not be that (one) wishes...'. به گیتی درنگ = 'to linger/delay in the world' (به + گیتی = 'in/within the world', درنگ = 'tarry/delay').
segments.s314-g85: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said' (used to give an order). تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'), and کار برساختند is the content clause: کار 'the work' + برساختند 'they built/did' (past plural).
segments.s314-g86: دو زورق is the direct object 'two boats'. به آب اندر (double preposition) means 'into the water' (به…اندر = 'into/onto'). انداختند is the past plural verb 'they cast/put/launched'. The order (object first) is normal in Persian narrative style.
segments.s314-g87: شناسای کشتی is an adjectival phrase 'knowing the ship / acquainted with the ship' (شناسای used attributively). هر آنکس که بود is a free relative 'anyone who was (whoever he was)' with بود as past 'was'. Together they introduce the person described.
segments.s314-g88: که (relative 'who/that') introduces the clause 'who showed bravery'. بر ژرف دریا = 'upon the deep sea' (بر = on/upon; ژرف دریا = the deep sea). دلیری نمود = 'displayed/manifested bravery' with نمود as the past verb 'showed/displayed.'
segments.s314-g161: چو بشنید is a temporal clause: چو = 'when' (classical conjunction) + بشنید, an older past form of شنیدن, meaning 'he heard.' The name phrase گستهم نوذر immediately follows as the subject (a personal name with patronymic) so the whole chunk reads 'When Gostaham (son of) Nowzar heard...'. که that begins the subordinate clause introduced next.
segments.s314-g162: بدان is the fused prepositional phrase به + آن = 'to/at that.' شارستان = 'city, town.' پدر here functions as 'founder' or 'father (i.e. originator)'—a common archaic use—so پدر کرد راه literally 'the father made (the) way' i.e. 'his father had founded it.' کرد is the past of کردن 'to make/do.' راه ('road/way') completes the verbal idea; overall: 'that the king had gone to that city which his father had founded.'
segments.s314-g163: پذیره شدش is a passive/periphrastic expression: پذیره (reception) + شدش (archaic past construction with the clitic ش referring to 'him' — here meaning 'he was received'). با سپاهی گران = 'with a heavy/mighty army,' where سپاهی ('army/soldiers') is modified postpositively by گران ('heavy/important' in epic style).
segments.s314-g164: ز ایران = 'from Iran' (ز is the poetic form of از). بزرگان و کنداوران lists the groups doing the receiving: بزرگان 'the great ones/nobles' and کنداوران (a group-name; here literal sense aligns with 'excavators' in the provided options). و simply coordinates the two nouns.
segments.s314-g165: چو = 'when' introduces the temporal clause. از دور = 'from afar' (prepositional phrase placed before the verb for emphasis). دید is the simple past 'he saw.' افسر و تاج شاه is the object: افسر (helmet) and تاج (crown) in genitival relation to شاه ('the king') — classical noun + possession follows.
segments.s314-g166: پیاده (on foot) + بپیمود (archaic past of پیمودن with the verbal prefix بـ, 'he traversed/covered') forms the verbal phrase 'he went on foot/covered (the road).' فراوان here is adverbial or intensifying 'a great deal/much.' راه is the object of پیمودن ('the road/ground' that was covered).
segments.s314-g167: همه یکسره places the adverbial/quantifier before the verb: همه = 'everyone/all,' یکسره = 'altogether/at once.' خواندند is the past plural 'they shouted/called out' (in epic usage it can mean 'proclaimed' or 'cried'). آفرین is the interjection/noun 'bravo/praise' that they uttered.
segments.s314-g168: بر here functions as the preposition 'to/for' in the idiom آفرین بر فلان ('praise to X'). آن = 'that' (demonstrative). دادگر شهریار is a compound attributive: دادگر 'just (one)/just ruler' modifying شهریار 'king/sovereign.' زمین 'of the earth/land' further qualifies the title ('sovereign of the earth').
segments.s314-g137: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. آمد is the past 3rd-singular 'came'. بدان is the archaic prepositional sequence ب + دان meaning 'to that' (ب = to/into, دان here fused as part of the demonstrative/locative), and شارستان is the noun 'city'. پدر (the subject 'the father') comes at the end; the clause order is Conj + Verb + prepositional phrase + subject, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s314-g138: دو رخساره is a numeral + noun 'two faces/cheeks'. پر is an adjectival element meaning 'full (of)' that takes a following noun (آب here = 'water', figuratively 'tears'), so پر آب = 'full of tears'. و links the next adjectival phrase خسته جگر, where خسته ('weary') precedes جگر ('liver' used idiomatically for 'heart/seat of feeling'). Both descriptive phrases modify the previously mentioned پدر.
segments.s314-g139: به جایی 'to a place' begins the locative phrase; که is the relative/connector 'where/that' introducing the relative clause. گرسیوز is the proper name 'Garsivaz' (subject of the embedded clause). بدنشان is the possessed noun 'their body/bodies' (بدن + enclitic شان 'their').
segments.s314-g140: گروی here is an archaic/poetic verbal/adjectival form; in context it describes the state of the bodies (rendered idiomatically as 'bound' or 'in captivity'). به نفرین مردم is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/under the people's curse' (به = by/toward, نفرین = curse, مردم = the people). کشان is the present-participial/active adjectival -ان form meaning 'dragging' (i.e. 'dragged'). The whole segment yields 'their bodies, bound, dragged under the people's curse.' (Note: گروی is archaic/poetic and does not match the modern senses in the provided lemma list, so its sense is marked as unknown.)
segments.s314-g141: سر شاه ایران is the direct object phrase 'the head of the Shah of Iran' (noun + title + genitive place-name). بریدند is the past 3rd-plural 'they cut (off)'. خوار is a postpositive adjective meaning 'despised' or 'humiliated' and characterizes the result/state following the action; subject is omitted (impersonal 'they' doing the cutting).
segments.s314-g142: بیامد is past 3rd-singular 'came'. بدان جایگه is the prepositional locution 'to that place' (ب + دان = 'to that', جایگه = 'place'). شهریار is the nominative appositive/subject 'the sovereign' (here the person who came). The verb precedes the subject (poetic inversion): 'the sovereign came to that place.'
segments.s314-g143: همی is a habitual/continuous particle meaning 'kept on' or 'continually'. ریخت is the past of ریختن 'to throw/pour' used here with the literal sense of scattering dust. بر سر 'on the head' is the prepositional phrase indicating the target. از آن تیره خاک literally 'from that dark dust/soil' — تیره خاک is mournful/dark dust used in mourning rituals. The clause describes the action of sprinkling dust on one's head in grief.
segments.s314-g144: همی کرد is again the habitual construction 'kept doing' (همی + کرد). روی here functions as one object/complement of کرد and means 'face/turning of the face' (i.e. he kept turning/covering his face). و coordinates the second action: بر خویش چاک — بر + خویش ('upon himself', with خوی + enclitic ش = 'his own') and چاک 'a tear/gash' used as a verbalized result 'to rend/tear' (i.e. tore at his garments). Grammatically, کرد governs both coordinated actions ('کرد روی' and 'کرد بر خویش چاک').
segments.s314-g193: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). او is the third-person subject 'he'. راه here means 'way/affairs' (metaphorical) and تو دادگر is a vocative insertion 'you, O Just One' addressing the addressee; نسپرد is the past negative of سپردن ('he did not entrust/commit'). Grammatically the clause says 'who did not entrust his way to you.'
segments.s314-g194: کسی را + ... نشمرد forms a negative idiom: نشمرد ('he did not count/consider') takes کسی را (object marked 'anyone/any person') and the prepositional phrase ز گیتی 'from/among the world'. به کس is idiomatic in this poetic line ('as/to any person' or 'as anyone'); the whole means 'he did not regard anyone in the world as anyone' — i.e. he respected no one. Note را is the definite/object marker attached to کسی.
segments.s314-g195: تو دانی ('you know') is the present indicative addressed to 'you'. که introduces the content clause. او نیست بر داد و راه uses نیست بر + nouns to mean 'he is not for/does not uphold' — so 'he does not uphold justice (داد) and the right way (راه)'. The preposition/particle بر here marks the domain in which he fails to stand.
segments.s314-g196: بسی is an intensifier 'much/many'; ریخت is the past of ریختن used as 'poured' or 'shed'; خون سر literally 'blood of heads' = 'blood of (people's) heads' and idiomatically 'bloodshed' or 'blood of the innocent'; بیگناه qualifies 'innocent'. Together: 'he has shed much innocent blood.'
segments.s314-g197: مگر here introduces a wish/conditional 'if only' or 'would that'. باشدم is an archaic/poetic first‑person subjunctive/wish form 'I were / I might be'. دادگر modifies یک خدای: 'a just (one), a single God' — hyperbolic phrasing meaning 'if only I were a just sovereign/judge.'
segments.s314-g198: به نزدیک = 'near/close to'. آن بدکنش = 'that ill‑doer/evildoer' (آن demonstrative + بدکنش 'ill‑doer'). رهنمای is a nominal predicate 'a guide'; it links back to باشدم so the clause reads 'to be a guide near that evildoer' — the speaker wishes to be such a guide.
segments.s314-g199: تو دانی که 'you know that' introduces the clause. من خود emphatically 'I myself'. سراینده‌ام is a noun 'poet' with the first‑person suffix -ام 'I am' — 'I am (the) poet.' In classical/poetic Persian the suffix can attach to the last predicate of a series but here marks the first predicate.
segments.s314-g200: پرستنده آفریننده‌ام combines two nouns: پرستنده 'worshipper' and آفریننده 'the Creator' with the final -ام understood as 'I am'. Read idiomatically as 'I am a worshipper of the Creator.' Classical Persian often places the genitive/appositive noun next to the worshipper and closes the clause with the copular suffix.
segments.s314-g89: بفرمود is a past, honorific verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose or reported command ('that / so that'). بادبان is the object 'the sail' and appears before the verb (typical Persian V-final order). برکشید here is the verb in a jussive/subjunctive/commanded sense after تا: 'be hoisted / be raised'.
segments.s314-g90: به دریای بی‌مایه is a directional phrase: به + دریای = 'into/towards the sea' and بی‌مایه is an adjectival modifier 'without substance/insipid'. اندر کشید is a verbal phrase of motion where اندر (in/into) emphasizes entry and کشید denotes pulling/steering/sailing into (subject omitted; implied crew/ship).
segments.s314-g91: همان راه دریا is a demonstrative + noun phrase 'that very sea-route'. به یک ساله راه is a measure/appositive: 'a one-year journey' (ساله used adjectivally to mean 'year-long'); word order is somewhat appositional, repeating راه for emphasis.
segments.s314-g92: چنان تیز شد is 'became so/that swift' (چنان = 'so/such', تیز = 'swift'), with شد the past copula/verb 'became'. باد follows as the agent/subject 'the wind' (common Persian order: predicate before subject). در هفت ماه is a temporal phrase 'within/in seven months.'
segments.s314-g93: که introduces the clause; آن شاه و لشکر is the noun phrase 'that king and (his) army'. بدین سو is a deictic directional phrase 'to this side' (here written as ب + دین + سو in the segmentation) and گذشت is the past verb 'passed'.
segments.s314-g94: که again links the explanatory clause. از باد = 'because of / from the wind'. کژ آستی‌تر is a comparative description: کژ 'askew/crooked' + آستی‌تر 'more slow/less violent', yielding 'more off-course/askew' or 'more unsteady'. نگشت is negative past 'did not become/turn' (here: 'did not become more so').
segments.s314-g95: سپهدار لشکر is a title + possessee phrase 'the commander of the army'. به خشکی indicates direction 'onto dry land' (خشکی = 'dry land/dryness') and کشید is the past verb 'drew/hailed' (here used for hauling people/ships ashore; subject/action objects are contextually understood).
segments.s314-g96: ببستند is a plural past 'they tied up / they secured' (used of mooring ships). کشتی is the object 'the ship(s)'. و هامون بدید follows with 'and (they) saw the plain'—بدید is past 'saw' and comes after the noun as normal Persian V-final order.
segments.s314-g225: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here marking items in a list). گستردنیها is a plural nominalized adjective meaning 'things to be spread' (carpets, cloths, table‑spreads) and follows Persian noun phrase ordering; و is the simple conjunction 'and'; آلات چین literally 'implements of China' (چین = China) — in older Persian the place/name follows the noun it qualifies.
segments.s314-g226: ز again = 'from'; چیزی is the indefinite pronoun 'something/anything'; که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'; خیزد is the classical 3rd‑singular present (from خاست/خیز) used here to mean 'springs up / arises'; ز مکران زمین = 'from the land of Makran' (مکران a place name + زمین 'land').
segments.s314-g227: ز is a prepositional marker (here 'with' / 'by means of' in poetic listing). گاوان is the plural 'oxen/cattle'; گردونکشان is a compound epithet 'sky‑(or heaven‑)pullers' modifying گاوان (epithetic modifier follows the noun); چل is the numeral 'forty' (here part of the larger quantity 'چل هزار' = forty thousand, a stock poetic numeral).
segments.s314-g228: همی is the archaic/narrative particle often used with past verbs to indicate continual or emphatic action; راند is the past of راندن 'drove / caused to be driven'; پیش here means 'before / in the presence of' (i.e. in front of); اندرون 'inner quarters' and شهریار 'king' form the phrase 'the king's inner apartments' (older Persian places the modifier after the noun).
segments.s314-g229: همی here again is the narrative particle preposing the speech-verb; گفت is the past verb 'said' (note: the provided lemma senses list does not include the verbal sense, so that sense is used); هرگز 'never' precedes کسی 'anyone' for negative emphasis; پیش از این is a temporal phrase 'before this / previously'.
segments.s314-g230: ندید is the past negative 'did not see'; و links the two negative assertions; نبد is an archaic form meaning 'there was not / did not exist' (negated existential), followed by خواسته (past participle/noun 'a desire' or 'that which was desired'); بیش از این is the comparative 'more than this / greater than this' — together: 'there had not been a desire greater than this.'
segments.s314-g231: سپه بود = 'there was an army' (سپه 'army' + بود 'was'); چندان is a degree adverb 'so much/so great'; که introduces the result clause 'that'; بر کوه و دشت = 'over mountain and plain' with بر = 'over/on' and the two locatives listed in parallel.
segments.s314-g232: همی again marks the narrative/progressive sense; ده شب و روز = 'ten nights and days' (a fixed time expression meaning ten days and nights); لشکر is the subject 'the host/army' and گذشت is the past verb 'passed / marched on' — the verb at the end is the normal classical Persian word order.
segments.s314-g153: بر امید آن is a prepositional phrase: بر + امید + آن = 'on the hope of that / in hope of that'. کش here functions as the definite person/object targeted (literary use; 'that man/that one'). به چنگ آوردم is the idiom 'به چنگ آوردن' = 'to seize, to get into one's grasp'; آورم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form used here with a future/performative force ('I will seize'). Word order is topical prepositional phrase → object → verb, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s314-g154: جهان پیش او is 'the world before him' (جهان = object, پیش او = prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before him'). تار و تنگ is an idiomatic pair meaning 'tight, narrow, constrained' modifying how the world is presented. آورم repeats the same 1st-person verb 'I bring/put' (present/subjunctive used narratively). The clause thus completes the action started in the previous segment.
segments.s314-g155: از آن پس is a temporal adverbial 'from that time on / thereafter'. (ب + آن) here appears as the fused locative/pronominal phrase 'بدان' = 'on/unto that' (به + آن). گنج is the noun 'treasure'. بنهاد is past tense (preterite) of نهادن 'to place/put/establish' — here 'he set' or 'he fixed'. سر in this context is figurative 'heart/inner thought/intent' (he set his heart/mind on it). The clause therefore means 'from then he fixed his heart on that treasure.'
segments.s314-g156: که is the relative/linking particle 'that' introducing the content clause. مادر بدو یاد کرد is literally 'the mother gave a reminder to him' = 'his mother reminded him'; بدو = 'to him'. از پدر is an ablative 'about/from his father' (she reminded him of something that came from his father). کرد is the past tense of کردن used as a light verb 'made/reminded'.
segments.s314-g157: در گنج بگشاد = 'he opened the treasury' (در + گنج locating the action; بگشاد is the past of گشودن 'to open' used here in the simple past). و links the two actions. روزی بداد literally 'gave روزی' — روزی in epic usage means 'sustenance/provisions'; بداد is past 'he gave/distributed' (داد used as a past-tense light verb). The clause describes opening the treasure and giving out supplies.
segments.s314-g158: دو هفته is a duration 'two weeks'. در آن شارستان = 'in that city/town' (در + آن + شارستان). بود شاد is the past-tense copula بود 'he was' + predicate شاد 'happy/joyful' describing his state during that period. Word order places duration and location before the copular clause, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s314-g159: به رستم = indirect object 'to Rostam'. دو صد = 'two hundred' (cardinal number). بدره دینار is a coin-expression; دینار is the currency noun and بدره here qualifies the coins (a poetic term; the exact numismatic sense is specialized). داد is the past 'gave' (3rd person past). The clause is a simple ditransitive 'gave X (to Rostam) Y (two hundred dinars)'.
segments.s314-g160: همان گیو را = 'that same Giv' with را marking the definite direct object/topic (here Giv is the beneficiary/recipient). چیز بسیار داد = 'gave many things' (چیز 'thing' as countable item, بسیار 'many/much' qualifying it), and داد is again past 'gave'. The clause repeats the act of generous giving, focusing on Giv as receiver.
segments.s314-g145: بمالید is a past tense verb (3rd person) placed before its subject in the classical/epic word order (V S). رستم is the subject that follows the verb. بر آن خاک روی is a prepositional/locative phrase (بر + demonstrative آن + خاک) with روی (‘face’) as the part of the body that is rubbed into the dust.
segments.s314-g146: This clause gives two short predicates. بنفرید is used as a stative/past result meaning ‘became purple/flushed’. بر جان ناکس is an oath formula meaning ‘by the life of the wretch’ (بر + جان + modifier ناکس). گروی is the verb (classical form of گرویدن) meaning ‘swore/took an oath’, concluding the clause.
segments.s314-g147: همی here is the customary narrative/adverbial particle that accompanies verbs in epic Persian (marks continuous or emphatic speech); گفت is the past-tense verb ‘said’. کیخسرو‌ای is the vocative form (name + ای) and شهریار is an appositive/title addressed to him ('sovereign').
segments.s314-g148: مرا is the first‑person object (to/for me) placed before the verb for emphasis; ماندی is 2nd person past ‘you remained’ (addressing Kay Khosrow). در جهان is a locative phrase ‘in the world’ and یادگار is the predicative noun meaning ‘memento’ or ‘remembrance’ (i.e. ‘you remain for me as a memento in the world’).
segments.s314-g149: نماندم is past/perfect first person with the negative prefix ن‑ (‘I did not / I will not remain’ in contextual/epic sense). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’ used here with a causal sense ‘because of’; کین تو = ‘your hatred’. مانند چیز is a simile 'like a thing' (مانند + چیز), i.e. 'like a mere object'.
segments.s314-g150: به رنج literally ‘into/with suffering’; اندرم is a fused form meaning ‘in me/within me’ (I carry/feel). تا is the temporal/concessive 'as long as', and جهانست is an archaic fused copular expression (جهان + است) meaning 'while the world exists'. نیز adds 'also/too' or emphatic continuation.
segments.s314-g151: بپرداختم is first‑person past; in epic usage پرداختن can mean ‘to deal with/overthrow/take care of’ an enemy’s seat of power — here it governs تخت افراسیاب, 'Afrasiyab's throne' as its object. The verb plus object expresses a completed action by the speaker.
segments.s314-g152: از این پس is an adverbial phrase 'from this time onward'. The paired نه ... نه ... is a correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'; آرام جویم uses جویم (1st person present/subjunctive 'I seek') with آرام ('peace/rest'), and خواب is the second noun in the negation ('sleep'). Together: 'from now on I will seek neither peace nor sleep.'
segments.s314-g129: پیاده شد is adjective + شد used idiomatically to mean 'he dismounted / got off (his mount)'. از باره is a prepositional phrase 'from the load/burden' (باره = baggage, load). بردش is an archaic past form of بردن with the enclitic object -ش (= 'him/it') meaning 'took/removed him/it' (the exact lemma senses given do not include this verbal sense, so the item functions here as a past transitive with clitic). نماز is the object 'prayer' which here appears before its verb because the verb appears in the next segment (poetic SOV/inverted order): together نماز گرفت(ش) = 'made him pray / compelled him to pray / caused the rite'.
segments.s314-g130: گرفتش is گرفت + enclitic -ش 'him' and is a past tense verb meaning 'seized / took / caused (him to do)'; the enclitic marks the object. به بر is a two-part prepositional phrase 'to/into (the presence of)' where به = 'to' and بر = a directional 'upon/toward' emphasizing motion into the addressee's presence. شاه is the head noun 'king' and گردن‌فراز is an epithet 'proud/boastful' postmodifying the king (typical in epic poetry).
segments.s314-g131: بگفت is the past-reporting verb 'he said / he told'. آن شگفتی is the direct object 'that marvel / that wonder' with آن pointing back to the thing related; که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. دید is the past tense 'he saw / beheld'. اندر آب is the locative phrase 'in the water' (اندر = 'in/within', آب = 'water').
