segments.s314-g132: ز here functions as the poetic/prepositional 'of/from' introducing the content of the marvel. گم بودن is a nominal clause formed from the adjective گم 'lost' + بودن 'to be', i.e. 'the being lost' or 'the fact of having been lost' (a common Persian way to form 'the loss/disappearance of ...'). جادو افراسیاب is a genitive/possessive ordering meaning 'the magic of Afrasiab' (poetic word order places the possessed after the possessor). The whole phrase reports 'that Afrasiab's magic had been lost'.
segments.s314-g133: به چین is a locative phrase 'in/at Chin' (here Chin = the country, not a wrinkle). نیز is the adverb 'also / likewise' attached to the clause. مهمان is a predicative noun 'guest' and رستم is the subject; بماند (ماند with the prefix/auxiliary ب-) is the past/resultant sense 'remained' (verb placed at the end in SOV order typical of Persian).
segments.s314-g134: به یک هفته is a temporal expression 'after one week' (classical Persian often uses به + duration to indicate 'after / at the end of' a period). از چین = 'from Chin' gives the origin, and به ماچین = 'to Ma-chin' gives the destination. براند is the past tense of راندن 'to drive/lead' (3rd sg), so the clause means 'he drove/led (them) from Chin to Ma-chin.' Note: ماچین here is a toponym in the poem; the lemma senses supplied do not match a place-name, so the sense is marked accordingly.
segments.s314-g135: همی رفت is an iterative/progressive past 'he kept going / he went' with همی adding continuity or emphasis (common in epic style). سوی = 'toward(s)' marks direction and is followed by the name سیاووش (Siyâvash). گرد here functions adverbially as a directional/attendant particle meaning 'toward / about' (poetic usage) and completes the phrase 'he went toward Siyâvash.'
segments.s314-g136: به ماه ... مذ روز ... is a calendrical temporal phrase: به ماه + name = 'in the month X', where ماه here is 'month' (not 'moon'); سفندار is the month-name (Sefandār / Spandarmad). مذ is a classical particle 'aforementioned / said' linking the month to the following day-name; روز = 'day' and ارد is the day-name. The construction reads 'in the month Sefandār, on the day Ord.' (These are old/Persian calendar names common in epic diction.)
segments.s314-g65: به راهی is an adverbial prepositional phrase indicating location/direction ('along/to a road'). که introduces a relative clause describing that road: لشکر همی برگذشت = 'the army was passing along it.' همی is an archaic/progressive particle often used in epic poetry to give a continuous or iterative sense to the verb; برگذشت is the past of 'pass by.'
segments.s314-g66: در و دشت is the compound subject 'town (lit. gates/settlements) and plain' meaning 'everywhere.' یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly' modifying the predicate. چو بازار گشت is a simile + verb: چو = 'like/as', بازار = 'market', گشت = past of گشتن meaning 'became/turned into.' The clause thus says the places 'became entirely like a marketplace.'
segments.s314-g67: به here introduces a locative list 'on/in' the places that follow: کوه و بیابان و جای = 'mountain and desert and place' (i.e., everywhere). نشست is the past form 'sat'/'were seated' (نشستن), used here to describe people being present in those locations.
segments.s314-g68: کسی را نبد کس is an archaic negative-existential turn: نبد (old form of نبود) = 'there was not / there was none,' so the phrase means 'there was no one' (emphatically). که introduces a relative clause describing that non‑existent exception: بگشاد دست = 'opened his hand' (i.e., gave or stretched out his hand). Put together: there was no one who did not open his hand (so everyone opened their hand).
segments.s314-g69: بزرگان = 'the great ones' (nobles/leaders) is the subject. ابا here denotes reluctance/refusal (used adverbially: 'though reluctant'). هدیه و با نثار lists the means/accompaniment 'with gift and with offering' (نثار = offerings/tributes). The whole phrase describes the nobles together with the manner and items involved.
segments.s314-g70: پذیره شدندی is a past passive/mediopassive plural: پذیره = 'reception/acceptance', شدندی = 'they were made / they were received' (old epic plural ending). بر شهریار indicates the recipient/agental locus 'by/at the king' (بر here = 'toward/to' in the sense 'in the presence of / before the king').
segments.s314-g71: چو = 'when.' خلعت فراز refers to the 'robes of honour' (خلعت) with فراز ('on high' / splendid) qualifying them; آمدیشان is a poetic past form meaning 'came to them' / 'were brought to them' (آمد + the clitic ایشان). ز گنج = 'from the treasury/treasure.' Together: 'when robes of honour were brought to them from the treasury.'
segments.s314-g72: نهشتی is an obscure/archaic form here (referring to the stored/hidden hoard or a related notion) and does not match the provided sense glosses cleanly; که introduces a relative clause. با او = 'with it' (referring back to the treasure/hoard). برفتی is second-person past 'you carried/removed/went with' and به رنج = 'with hardship/with toil.' The clause as a whole qualifies the treasury: 'the hoard which you had taken from it with difficulty.'
segments.s314-g217: به چین و به مکران زمین is a sequence of directional phrases 'to China and to the land of Makran' (زمین here = 'land/country'). دست یاز is a classical verb phrase literally 'stretch/put forth the hand' (دست + یازیدن) meaning 'to reach out or lay hold (on)'. The verb follows its directional complements in typical classical word order: 'to China and Makran's land he reached out his hand.'
segments.s314-g218: به هر سو ('to every direction/quarter') is an adverbial phrase. فرستاده here functions as 'messengers' (or the sent parties) — a noun/participial form; the clause means he dispatched messengers. و نامه ساز coordinates a second action: نامه ساز literally 'maker/producer of letters' i.e. 'he composed/wrote letters' (classical noun + verb sense where ساز stands for 'making' or 'composer' in context). The clause therefore lists his actions of sending envoys and composing letters to the four corners.
segments.s314-g219: همی is a habitual/continuous marker placed before the verb to show ongoing action. جوی is the present stem of جستن (to seek) and here functions as a 3rd-person imperfective ‘keeps seeking’. ز + افراسیاب is a prepositional source phrase ‘from Afrasiab’ and آگهی is the direct object ‘news/information’; poetic word order allows the object and source to follow the verb.
segments.s314-g220: مگر introduces a fearing/conditional sense here (‘lest’ / ‘in case’). The sequence ز و should be read as ز + او (i.e. ‘from him’); the printed و here represents the pronoun او rather than the conjunction و. شود is the present/subjunctive-type form of شدن ‘become/come to be’ indicating a possible result, and روی گیتی تهی is a predicate phrase ‘the face of the world [be] empty’ (روی = ‘face’, گیتی = ‘world’, تهی = ‘empty’), with the adjective following the noun as is common in classical usage.
segments.s314-g221: و begins the clause 'and.' زآن جایگه = 'from that place/position' (زآن = 'from that', جایگه = 'place/rank'). خواسته is the past/perfect participle used as the main verb here meaning 'he demanded/asked for'; هرچه بود = 'whatever there was' (هرچه 'whatever' + بود past 'was'), acting as the object of خواسته.
segments.s314-g222: ز دینار وز گوهر lists the things he demanded: ز دینار = 'from dinars' (i.e., dinar coins). وز is a poetic contraction (و + ز) meaning 'and of / and from' before گوهر ('gems, jewels'). نابسود is a negative/adjectival predicate describing his appetite as 'not sated' / 'unsparing' — here it qualifies his demands (he was not content with dinars and jewels).
segments.s314-g223: ز introduces another list of items 'from/among': ز مشک و پرستار و زرین ستام = 'musk and attendants and golden ستام.' The nouns are coordinated by و. پرستار in this context means 'attendant/servant' rather than a worshipper. زرین (golden) modifies ستام; ستام is treated as a noun here (likely a poetic item/tribute), so the whole phrase enumerates luxury goods and retinue.
segments.s314-g224: همان = 'the same' or 'those very' (referring back to the demanded goods). The following series جامه و اسب و تخت و غلام lists items in apposition: 'garments and horses and throne and slave(s),' joined by و in the usual Persian enumerative pattern.
segments.s314-g73: پذیره شدش is a poetic/periphrastic passive: پذیره (reception) + شد (happened) with the enclitic -ش referring to 'him' — literally 'a reception happened to him' = 'he was received/welcomed'. گیو is the agent/subject (placed after the verb, as is common in older/poetic Persian). با لشکری is a comitative/instrumental phrase 'with an army' describing the accompaniment or force with which the reception occurred.
segments.s314-g74: و is the coordinating 'and'. زآن شهر is a prepositional phrase 'from that city'. هر کس که is a free relative 'whoever / every person who'; the clause that follows omits an explicit copula (بود) by poetic ellipsis. بد مهتری is a noun phrase where بد ('poor/ill') qualifies مهتری ('preeminence, rank'), so together meaning 'of inferior rank' or 'of lesser preeminence.'
segments.s314-g75: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دید is the simple past 'he saw'. آن سر و فره sرفراز is a coordinated noun phrase: آن 'that' + سر 'head' (here literal/physical head, honorably used) + و فره 'and farr' (فره = the traditional notion of divine glory) with the adjective سرفراز following and qualifying the whole phrase (postpositive adjective common in poetry).
segments.s314-g76: پیاده شد is an intransitive periphrasis 'he became on foot' = 'he dismounted / went down to the ground'. و is 'and'. برد here means 'took/held/carried' in the sense of performing/bringing something; پیشش ('in his presence' / 'before him') places the action in front of that person. نماز is the object 'prayer' (ritual prayer); the order برد پیشش نماز is a poetic word order (object and adverbial can be reordered for meter).
segments.s314-g77: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign / king'. بسیار is an adverb 'much' or 'greatly' modifying the verb. بنواختشان is past tense of نواختن with the prefixed بـ (poetic form) + the object enclitic -شان 'them' — literally 'he played for them' (i.e., made music or sounded the instruments for them).
segments.s314-g78: به رسم کیان is a prepositional phrase 'in accordance with the custom/ceremony of the Kians' (کیان = the royal house/kings). جایگه is 'place / position / rank'. ساختشان is the past of ساختن 'made/established/appointed' with object enclitic -شان 'them' — here used in the sense 'he assigned them places' rather than literal building.
segments.s314-g79: چو is 'when'. خسرو is the subject 'Khosrow'. به نزدیک کشتی is a locative phrase 'to / near the ship' (به + نزدیک + کشتی). رسید is the simple past 'arrived' or 'reached' — so 'when Khosrow came close to the ship' (literal word order: temporal marker + subject + locative + verb).
segments.s314-g80: فرود آمد is a compound verbal expression 'he descended / alighted / disembarked' (فرود = descent, آمد = came). و is 'and'. بادبان برکشید is transitive: بادبان 'the sail' (object) + برکشید 'he hoisted/raised' (بر- prefix + کشیدن) — 'he raised/hoisted the sail.'

sentences.s314-s125: And from that place he set out to go; he went with his heart's desire, the king happy.
sentences.s314-s89: Among the Turks, whoever grew arrogant and over-proud needed no one's favour.
sentences.s314-s90: By day, bright as it was, and at the time of sleep he constantly sought news from Afrasiab.
sentences.s314-s91: None of them gave any sign of him; they did not mention him in the world either.
sentences.s314-s92: One night the sovereign washed his head and body; he withdrew afar with the Zand book and remained.
sentences.s314-s13: The king was in the Gank citadel for a year, living in tranquillity and with wine-companions.
sentences.s314-s14: The world was like a charming paradise; it was full of flower-gardens, gardens, and orchards.
sentences.s314-s15: The king's heart would not consent to going away; he remained in the Gank (citadel) victorious and joyful.
sentences.s314-s16: All the champions and the army of Iran went at once toward the king.
sentences.s314-s93: All night long he remained before the Creator, weeping with his head upon the ground.
sentences.s314-s94: He kept saying, 'This helpless servant always has his soul full of pain.'
sentences.s314-s95: He saw not a sign of Afrasiab in all the mountains, rivers, wastes, and waters.
sentences.s314-s96: He kept saying, 'O just judge, you have given me pride, strength, and glory.'
sentences.s314-s9: They searched the plains, gardens, and houses, and they took guides on every side.
sentences.s314-s10: The seeker kept going on as if without them, until perhaps from him they might find some sign of a place.
sentences.s314-s11: When they hastened swiftly to search for him, they found many of his people.
sentences.s314-s12: They killed many innocent people; no sign came from the king’s injustice.
sentences.s314-s29: At that moment, when the rooster woke, the sound of the war-drum rose from the portal.
sentences.s314-s30: The hastening army and path-seekers turned their faces toward the desert.
sentences.s314-s31: All the renowned men of every land went wherever there was bad leadership.
sentences.s314-s32: They brought the stews near the king, who was at the sovereign's gate with the host.
sentences.s314-s25: They decked his body in a robe of honour; they summoned the marzban from the fortress.
sentences.s314-s26: Thus he said, 'Kaydar, remain in joy; cast out from your heart the suspicious thought.'
sentences.s314-s27: They bestowed so much that even the ill-wisher was adorned with horses and treasure.
sentences.s314-s28: The whole city became prosperous because of them, for they were supplied with attendants, a throne, and helmets.
sentences.s314-s17: If the king's heart does not stir from his place, no counsel will come to the city of Iran.
sentences.s314-s18: Truly, the ill-minded Afrasiab has gone off to that side, to the sea.
sentences.s314-s19: Thus (he is) an old man at the court of King Kavus — neither throne and glory, nor treasure and army (are with him).
sentences.s314-s20: If he turns toward Iran full of hatred, who will be the guardian of the land of Iran?
sentences.s314-s5: He saw that heart-delighting garden of Paradise; his cruel men were like the lamps of Paradise.
sentences.s314-s6: In every corner there were springs and rose-gardens; the ground was hyacinths and the boughs of the land of nightingales.
sentences.s314-s7: Everyone who had put this (in place) kept saying: 'Let us remain here as well, happy until death.'
sentences.s314-s8: And after that he commanded, 'Awake, O king; seek the Turanian army.'
sentences.s314-s53: He dismounted from his horse, kissed the ground, and uttered praise to the king.
sentences.s314-s54: Everyone—both the swift and those of Makran—arrayed themselves, and from every quarter they summoned peacemakers.
sentences.s314-s55: Every road and off-road rang with the lute's song; you said the air became the warp, the strings the weft.
sentences.s314-s56: They hung brocade on the wall and scattered dirhams and sugar beneath the feet.
sentences.s314-s85: He told Gostaham to sit down; all the way joyful, his hand in hand.
sentences.s314-s86: They drew, therefore, toward the silent paradise; they led the host near the king — full of color and radiance.
sentences.s314-s87: Loyalty was like a fruit-bearing tree; at every season a new fruit comes to its yield.
sentences.s314-s88: Not a single person rested from food and the chase — that one rider, that sovereign.
sentences.s314-s105: He remained for a year in the (silent) fortress, resting from the stir and the making of war.
sentences.s314-s106: When his stay inside Gung grew long, he came to Kavus in supplication.
sentences.s314-s107: He entrusted that land to Gostaham, from Quchghar as far as the shores of the Chinese Sea.
sentences.s314-s108: He gave Gostaham an immense army; to him he said, 'Be alert of heart and joyful.'
sentences.s314-s121: He ate and rested and stayed for a week; the second week he remained in his garments unsatisfied.
sentences.s314-s122: He came roaring to the fire‑temple; there was grief because a dragon had been formed.
sentences.s314-s123: which Tūr (of Fereydun) had raised; he had built inner palaces for it.
sentences.s314-s124: He distributed silver and gold to the mōbeds, and scattered some jewels on the fire.
sentences.s314-s61: Thereupon he made him chief of the land of Makran, bestowed on him many robes of honour, and praised him.
sentences.s314-s62: When he came from Makran and Turan to his own ranks and to Iran's proud warriors.
sentences.s314-s63: He was received by Rostam, Zal, and Sam — their warriors open‑hearted and joyful.
sentences.s314-s64: When Kay Khosrow appeared from afar, a proud horseman raised his canopy.
sentences.s314-s117: When dawn came and the vanguard moved forward, he reached the lodging, again and again.
sentences.s314-s118: A sign also came upon that; and until it reached Chāj, he hung the crown upon the ivory throne.
sentences.s314-s119: The king was inside Sogd for one week; all Sogd became well-wishers of the king.
sentences.s314-s120: From there he reached the city of Bukhara; from the army no one saw (it).
